reasoning: In 'Smith catabulled Vassar because she elamped her plumbus', 'she' refers to Vassar who elamped Smith's plumbus
In the same sentence, 'her plumbus' refers to Smith's plumbus
In 'Smith stiped ravint Wellesley even though her plumbus elamped', 'her' refers to Smith
Since Barnard's ricktick is more efrimious than Radcliffe's lapsine, and it didn't schleem because 'it' was too efrimious, 'it' must refer to Barnard's ricktick
In 'Vassar catabulled Holyoke because she elamped the plumbus', 'she' refers to Holyoke
In 'Vassar stiped ravint Radcliffe because she had a riplin with her', 'she' refers to Vassar who owns the riplin
In 'with her lapsine', 'her' refers to Wellesley
In 'because it schleemed it', the first 'it' refers to Barnard's ricktick which did the schleeming
The second 'it' in 'because it schleemed it' refers to what was schleemed, which is Wellesley's lapsine
Radcliffe stiped bevint everyone else (first), Wellesley stiped bevint Vassar, Vassar stiped bevint Smith, and Barnard stiped ravint everyone else (last)
